skill.name: code-style-enforcer
skill.description: Reviews code for project-specific style consistency and suggests formatting, naming, organization, and tooling improvements beyond standard linters.
skill.summary: Reviews code style against project conventions and recommends practical consistency improvements.
skill.author: CrazyDubya

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but manual review found they are Markdown examples, documentation links, or formatter command recommendations. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, malicious code execution, or unauthorized network behavior was found in the skill files. The remaining risk is low because users should still review suggested formatting and install commands before running them.

low_findings.title: Formatter and Linter Command Recommendations
low_findings.description: The skill recommends package installation and formatter commands for common ecosystems. These are legitimate style-enforcement suggestions, but users should review command scope before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit documentation examples for known formatting tools. The skill declares Read, Grep, Glob, and Edit only, so there is no direct shell execution capability.

low_findings.title: Documentation-Only Network References
low_findings.description: The static network findings are public style guide URLs and illustrative API examples. I found no evidence that the skill sends data to external services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are Markdown documentation and sample code blocks. No executable network client, credential handling, or exfiltration workflow is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive File Path Traversal Indicators
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts correspond to relative import examples and template placeholder paths. They do not read, write, or construct filesystem paths from user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged paths appear inside style examples and a style guide template. There is no filesystem API call or user-controlled path handling in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography static alerts are caused by Markdown filenames, headings, and ordinary style guide text. No evidence found for cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found Markdown documentation and references, not MD5, SHA1, or other weak cryptographic algorithm usage. The static signals do not map to executable code.

content.value_statement: Teams lose time when code follows mixed naming, formatting, and organization patterns. This skill reviews project conventions and suggests focused style fixes that linters may miss.
actual_capabilities: Finds common style configuration files such as ESLint, Prettier, EditorConfig, Black, RuboCop, and golangci-lint settings.
Samples existing files to infer naming, formatting, import, and file organization conventions.
Flags style issues that linters often miss, including inconsistent names, comments, error handling, and type usage.
Prioritizes style recommendations by consistency, readability, and nice-to-have cleanup impact.
Suggests standard formatter and linter tools for JavaScript, Python, Go, Ruby, and other supported ecosystems.
Can suggest an EditorConfig baseline when a project lacks shared editor settings.
limitations: It depends on readable project files and may miss conventions that are not present in the repository.
It does not replace language-specific linters, formatters, type checkers, or test suites.
It may recommend commands, but users should review commands before running them.
It focuses on style consistency, not deep correctness, performance, or security review.

use_cases.title: Review a Pull Request for Style Drift
use_cases.description: Compare changed files against project conventions and identify naming, import, formatting, and organization inconsistencies before merge.

use_cases.title: Prepare New Contributor Code
use_cases.description: Help new contributors align code with repository patterns before maintainers spend time on repeated style comments.

use_cases.title: Document Team Style Decisions
use_cases.description: Inspect existing patterns and turn repeated conventions into a practical style checklist or EditorConfig recommendation.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Style Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Use this when you want a quick style pass on a small change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these changed files for code style consistency. Focus on naming, formatting, imports, and comments. Give concise recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Match Existing Conventions
prompt_templates.scenario: Use this when a repository has implicit patterns but limited documentation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the project style files and nearby examples first. Then review my change and explain where it differs from existing conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Style Checklist
prompt_templates.scenario: Use this before onboarding contributors or preparing review guidance.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this repository and create a practical code style checklist. Include naming, imports, comments, file organization, and tooling recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Multi-Language Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Use this for repositories with several languages or mixed tooling.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit style consistency across the main languages in this repository. Separate findings by language, cite observed conventions, prioritize issues, and recommend automation where useful.

output_examples.input: Review a feature branch for style consistency before merge.
output: High priority: Function names in the new service do not match the existing verb-based pattern.
Medium priority: Import grouping differs from nearby modules and should be reordered.
Low priority: Two comments repeat what the code already says and can be removed.

output_examples.input: Create a style checklist for a repository without a formal guide.
output: Use existing formatter settings as the source of truth for indentation and line endings.
Keep service methods grouped by lifecycle order: create, read, update, delete.
Use explicit boolean prefixes for state checks, such as is, has, and should.

output_examples.input: Recommend style automation for a mixed JavaScript and Python project.
output: Use Prettier and ESLint for JavaScript formatting and lint enforcement.
Use Black and isort for Python formatting and import order.
Add shared editor settings so contributors get consistent whitespace behavior.

best_practices: Review project configuration and nearby files before making style recommendations.
Prioritize changes that improve consistency across similar code paths.
Use automated formatters for mechanical changes and reserve manual review for judgment calls.
anti_patterns: Do not rewrite working code only to match personal preferences.
Do not apply one language style guide to a project that already has clear local conventions.
Do not run suggested commands without reviewing their scope and effect first.

faq.question: Does this skill replace a formatter?
faq.answer: No. It complements formatters by reviewing naming, organization, comments, and project-specific conventions.

faq.question: Can it edit files directly?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill declares edit access, so it can apply style changes when the user asks for edits.

faq.question: Which languages does it cover?
faq.answer: It includes guidance for JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Ruby, Go, Java, C++, Rust, PHP, Swift, and Kotlin.

faq.question: Can it infer style without a written guide?
faq.answer: Yes. It samples existing files to infer common naming, formatting, import, and organization patterns.

faq.question: Will it run formatter commands automatically?
faq.answer: The skill documentation recommends common commands, but it does not declare shell execution access.

faq.question: Is it useful for code review?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed to identify style drift and consistency issues before code is merged.
